coredata.dc:title: Ultrasonic enhanced liquid-liquid interfacial reaction for improving the synthesis of Iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) for achieving superior photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zongbo
Song, Shiliang
Ding, Yan
Yu, Jianhai
Wu, Wenting
Li, Jin
Wang, Xin
Guo, Yong
Gong, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The precise and controllable preparation of carbon nanomaterials under mild conditions poses a great challenge, especially for metal-catalysed multiphase preparation. This work proposes an efficient method that utilizing high-density ultrasound to enhance the liquid-liquid interfacial reaction system. Iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) are successfully synthesized in such a normal temperature and atmospheric-pressure reaction condition. It is shown that transient cavitation provides a high-temperature and high-pressure microenvironment for the preparation of Fe-CDs. Moreover, the size of the reactant droplets is reduced from 200.0 ± 17.3 μm to 8.1 ± 2.9 μm owing to the acoustic flow and cavitation effects, which increases the specific surface area of the two reacting phases and improves the mass transfer coefficient by more than 252.0 %. As a result, the yield increases by more than an order of magnitude (from 0.7 ± 0.1 % to 11.9 ± 0.2 %) and the Fe doping rate reaches 20.9 %. The photocatalytic oxidation conversion of 1,4-Dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) using the obtained Fe-CDs is as high as 98.2 %. This research gives a new approach for the efficient and safe production of Fe-CDs, which is promising for industrial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Liquid-liquid reaction
Mass transfer enhancement
Ultrasonic cavitation
Fe-CDs
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of ultrasonic energy on synthesis
Effect of ultrasound on droplets and mass transfer
Photocatalytic properties of Iron-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the hydrophone experiment and measurement diagram.
(a) TEM Image of Fe-CDs. High-Resolution TEM, Particle Size Distribution Images of Fe-CDs (b) Raman spectra of Fe-CDs (c) FT-IR of Fe-CDs (d) XPS full spectrum of Fe-CDs.
Variation of FWHM and Particle Size Distribution of Fe-CDs under Different Ultrasonic Powers.

(a) The relationship between ultrasound energy at different ultrasonic power levels and the yield of Fe-CDs (b) The relationship between ultrasound energy at different ultrasonic power levels and the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of Fe-CDs.
(a) The Cross Section Total Energy Distribution of Ultrasonic Chemical Reaction Field (b) Transient Cavitation Energy Distribution in Cross Section of Ultrasonic Chemical Reaction Field.
Effect of ultrasonic power on droplet size and mass transfer coefficient in liquid–liquid systems.

(a) 1,4-DHP catalytic oxidation process (b) UV absorption for photocatalytic oxidation of 1,4-DHP by Fe CD (c) Efficiency of Fe CDs photocatalytic oxidation of 1,4-DHP (d) Stern-Volmer plots of Fe-CDs for different levels of DNT in acetonitrile (λex = 340 nm) (e) Stern-Volmer plots of Fe-CDs after addition of different amounts of DEA in acetonitrile (λex = 340 nm).
The Yield of Fe CDs in Different Method Systems.

head.ce:title: Ultrasonic enhanced liquid-liquid interfacial reaction for improving the synthesis of Iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) for achieving superior photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Mechanical and Electrical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Energy, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of New Energy, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The precise and controllable preparation of carbon nanomaterials under mild conditions poses a great challenge, especially for metal-catalysed multiphase preparation. This work proposes an efficient method that utilizing high-density ultrasound to enhance the liquid-liquid interfacial reaction system. Iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) are successfully synthesized in such a normal temperature and atmospheric-pressure reaction condition. It is shown that transient cavitation provides a high-temperature and high-pressure microenvironment for the preparation of Fe-CDs. Moreover, the size of the reactant droplets is reduced from 200.0 ± 17.3 μm to 8.1 ± 2.9 μm owing to the acoustic flow and cavitation effects, which increases the specific surface area of the two reacting phases and improves the mass transfer coefficient by more than 252.0 %. As a result, the yield increases by more than an order of magnitude (from 0.7 ± 0.1 % to 11.9 ± 0.2 %) and the Fe doping rate reaches 20.9 %. The photocatalytic oxidation conversion of 1,4-Dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP) using the obtained Fe-CDs is as high as 98.2 %. This research gives a new approach for the efficient and safe production of Fe-CDs, which is promising for industrial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been broadly applicated in the field of bioimaging, sensing and photocatalysis owing to their abundant surface functional groups and excellent photostability[1–3]. Nonetheless, the electron-hole recombination rate within pristine carbon dots is excessively rapid under light irradiation, which significantly undermines the photocatalytic efficiency of carbon dots. To solve this problem, doping is considered as an effective approach. Metal ions typically possess more electrons, unoccupied orbitals and a larger atomic radius in comparison with non-metallic atoms [4,5]. Metal-doped carbon dots (M−CDs) can significantly alter the electronic structure and surface active sites of CDs [6].Therefore, the electron transport properties and surface characteristics of CDs could be improved [7,8]. Among the metal elements, iron is earth-abundant and low-toxic, which is commonly used in metal catalyst research. Incorporating iron element into CDs to synthesize Fe-CDs is a promising way to ameliorate the electronic structure and increase surface active sites of CDs. The possibility of electron and energy transfer between Fe-CDs and substrates during photoreaction could be greatly enhanced [9,10].

As to the preparation of Fe-CDs, many attempts have been made to achieve superior photocatalytic properties. Yasaman et al. [11] prepared Fe-CDs with a particle size of approximately 15 nm using the hydrothermal method in which the synthesizing temperature and time were 200 °C and 15 h respectively. The selectivity of Fe-CDs for deferiprone (detection limits of 0.05–4.0 μg mL−1) was superior to that of zinc-, silver-, and gold-doped carbon dots. However, the above-obtained Fe-CDs had a small specific surface area, leading to relatively low mass transfer efficiency. Yang et al. [12] developed a new method combining hydrothermal synthesis and thermal decomposition for the synthesis of Fe-CDs. The precursors were hydrothermally synthesized at 180 °C for 12 h, followed by thermal decomposition at 350 °C for 2.5 h. The resultant Fe-CDs were only 3.2 nm. The enzyme affinity and catalytic rate of Fe-CDs surpassed those of metal-doped carbon dots such as Ce, Cr, Cu, etc., by more than tenfold. However, this synthesis process was time-consuming, complex, and the doping rate was very low (0.2 wt%). Xing et al. [13] successfully prepared Fe-CDs in only 4 h at 200 °C using a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. The synthesis time was dramatically reduced, but the doping rate was (1.89 %) was too low to meet the industrial applications demand although.

Generally, the hydrothermal method suffers from the long cycle time, the need of high temperature and high pressure reaction environment. The enhancement of iron doping rate is limited, so the synthesis of Fe-CDs is mainly restricted to laboratory studies. Yang et al. [14] investigated the synthesis of Fe-CDs by microwave irradiation. It was able to heat the precursor in a very short time and the preparation time could be shorted in (less than 1 h). However, the iron doping rate of this method was still low (less than 1.6 %). The inhomogeneous of microwave heating made the reaction uncontrollable and inhomogeneous, which leaded to the poor consistency of Fe-CDs.

Sonochemistry is a distinctive chemical synthesis system that utilizes ultrasonic cavitation to create an extreme reaction microenvironment at normal temperature and pressure. The acoustic streaming and cavitation effect of ultrasound can intensify the mass transfer in the reaction system. Bourlinos et al. [15] prepared Fe-CDs with a high doping rate (about 7.2 %) via tank-type ultrasonication. Owing to the ultrasonic field, the Fe-CDs were synthesized at normal temperature and pressure, which decreased the demands and risks of the reaction systems. However, the tank-type ultrasonic reaction utilize water as the ultrasound propagation medium, which reduced the acoustic energy density in the synthesis beaker. Especially for the solid–liquid reaction system, it was difficult to enhance the mass-transfer effect via the ultrasonic field, which resulted in long reaction time, poor homogeneity, and a low yield (about 0.07 %).

This study proposes an innovative method of preparing Fe-CDs by coupling a horn-based ultrasonic field and a liquid–liquid system (xylene and FeCl3 solution). The ultrasonic field generated by the horn has a high acoustic energy density. This creates a micro-environment with high temperature and pressure, which helps to synthesize materials more densely. Additionally, the large droplets are broken into smaller ones through the acoustic streaming and cavitation effects, which increasing the contact area between reactants. Consequently, the obtained Fe-CDs are small (the average diameter is about 5.3 nm) and with very good homogeneity. The doping rate and yield are higher than previous report, reaching 20.9 % and 11.9 ± 0.2 %, respectively. Furthermore, the catalytic performance of the Fe-CDs was studied through the photocatalytic oxidation reaction of 1,4-Dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP).

All the chemical reagents used in this research are analytical reagents (A.R.). Deionized water is used throughout the research. The reagents, including anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3), xylene (C8H10) (BTX), tetrahydrofuran (C4H8O) (THF), ethanol (CH3CH2OH), acetonitrile (CH3CN), nhexane (THF) (C4H14), 1,4-Dihydropyridine (1,4-DHP), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) and N, N-diethylaniline (DEA) are purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China, and no further purification is required.

To prepare the iron-containing carbon dots, 200 mg of FeCl3 (process of concentration determination as shown in Fig. S1 and Table S1) is dispersed in 10 ml of deionized water for 5 min by an ultrasonic cleaning tank with 100 W power. The dispersed phase (Aqueous ferric chloride) is slowly added to the continuous phase (Xylene) by the Syringe pump (SPC, Easypump, China) to prepare FeCl3 droplets. The flow rates are controlled by a controlling platform (SN-003, Shenchen pump 19 industry, China). Then, the solution containing dispersed FeCl3 droplets is mixed with 100 ml BTX, and place in the ultrasonic reactor for the emulsification treatment.

The ultrasonic source is provided by an ultrasonic nano-disperser SM-1500A (Shunmatech Nanjing). The electrical power input to the ultrasonic generator in the equipment ranged from 0-1500 W. And the experimental samples and results are distinguish using electrical power of ultrasonic. The acoustic cavitation power input into the reaction system is shown in Table S2. The reaction device and the cooling device to control the temperature are designed and manufactured independently. The water-cooling cycle acts on the top cover and the reaction vessel. The two serve slightly different purposes. The purpose of the former is to condense the volatile reaction solution. The purpose of the latter is to maintain the reaction temperature at about 50 °C. Iron-containing carbon dots are obtained by the liquid–liquid interfacial reaction, as shown in Fig. 1 . And the complete experimental procedure is shown in Fig. S15.

After the treatment, the unstratified product is centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min using a high-speed desktop centrifuge (TG 16-WS). The middle suspension is evaporated in a vacuum-drying oven. Fe-CDs are placed in n-hexane or deionized water, which is sonicated for 5 min and filtered 2–3 times to remove the excess FeCl3. The filtered solids are then dispersed in the THF solution, sonicate for 5 min, and filtere 3 to 4 times to remove the insoluble impurities. Finally, the filtrate is evaporated in a vacuum drying oven to obtain Fe-CDs, which are reddish-brown in color. The Fe-CDs prepared have excellent oil solubility, and good dispersion in acetone, chloroform, and tetrahydrofuran.

The yield of Fe-CDs can be calculated with the following equation:
M Y
( % )
=
M i
M 0
× 100 %

In this equation, MY represents Yield (%), Mi stands for Yield Actually Obtained (in mg), and M0 represents the Consumption of BTX (in mg) (Measured according to the instruction for the experiment 1 in revised supplementary material).

An ultrasonic measurement subsystem is constructed to investigate the influence of the energy in different segments of the ultrasonic field, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The hydrophone (TC4034; RESON A/S, Slangerup, Denmark) is employed to measure the acoustic signal within the reactor. The gathered information on cavitation noise spectrum is subsequently analyzed by the self-developed software to evaluate the strength of the cavitation field. The hydrophone (TC4034; RESON A/S, Slangerup, Denmark) is affixed using a self-developed three-dimensional stereotactic system mount [16]. The acoustic signals collected from the hydrophone are captured by a digital oscilloscope (DS2102, RIGOL, China). The right side of the figure shows a plot of the measurement trajectory and measuring point diagram of the hydrophone, which is composed of 8 concentric circles. The circles are with varying radii and contain 12 measurement points on each circumference.

Ultrasonic energy is commonly categorized as cavitation energy and non-cavitation energy. Cavitation energy is derived from the radiative forces and the acoustic microcurrents induced by steady-state cavitation, as well as the shockwaves generated by transient cavitation [17,18]. It is assessed through the Broadband Integrated Pressure (BIP). Non-cavitation energy, predominantly cause by ultrasonic vibrations [16], is evaluated using fundamental wave pressure (FWP).

The division of ultrasonic energy is accomplished through Matlab. It is segmented into the fundamental energy, the transient cavitation energy, and the steady-state cavitation energy. The dimensionless energy values can be obtained through integration, employing the Welch method for power spectrum estimation. In order to narrow the gap between these energy values, the logarithmic (lg) operation is performed on the energy values. The observability of the energy changes in the sound field is improved, which makes it easy to correlate with the yield and evenness.

The SB-5200 DTD (SCIENTZ) ultrasonic cleaning tank with a frequency of 40 kHz and a power range of 0–100 W is used to disperse the FeCl3. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images are acquired using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. Ultraviolet–visible (UV) spectra are made using a Lambda 750 (Perkin Elmer) spectrophotometer and a two-pass 10-mm quartz cuvette, with spectra collected from 200 to 800 nm. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra are collected on a NicoletTM iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific), employing a KBr tableting process with a range of 400–4000 cm−1 and a resolution of 1 cm−1. Photoluminescence (PL) is obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-6000) and a four-pass 10 mm quartz cuvette in the wavelength range of 300–800 nm on an RF-6000 (Shimadzu) spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is performed with an ESCALAB by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific). The content of elemental Fe in the ablated Fe-CDs is tested by inductively coupled plasma technique (ICP-MS).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements are carried out on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffraction diffractometer, which utilize a Co Kα (k = 0.17902 nm) X-ray source. The instrument is operated at 30 kV, utilizing a wide-angle X-ray diffraction (5°-90°) with a scanning speed of ≥10°·min−1 for powder diffraction. The XRD patterns show that the prepared Fe-CDs exhibited crystal structure spectral characteristics. The Raman spectrum, ranging from 3000 to 100 cm−1, is obtained using a LabRAM HR evolution (Japan-Horiba scientific) under laser excitation at 325 nm. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) measurements are carried out using an American-Agilent-7890B-5977A. The Iron doping concentration of Fe-CDs is studied by ICP-AES (Agilent 7700/7800, United State). And all the measurements are repeated 3–5 times.

Table 1 shows the comparison of yield and iron doping rate of Fe-CDs prepared in solid–liquid and liquid–liquid systems respectively. As seen, the yield of Fe-CDs prepared by immersion ultrasound with a solid–liquid system is about 1.0 %, which is 46.3 % higher than that of tank ultrasound [15], and the preparation time is shortened from 24 h to 3 h. This is mainly attributed to the high-density acoustic energy of immersion ultrasound, which strengthens the mass transfer and reaction process. However, in the solid–liquid system, the iron doping rates of the synthesized Fe-CDs by the tank ultrasound and immersed ultrasound are similar (7.2 % and 7.6 % respectively), which indicates that ultrasonic energy intensity is not the main affecting factor for the doping rate.

Significantly, the yield of Fe-CDs prepared in the liquid–liquid system with immersion ultrasound is as high as 11.9 ± 0.2 %, which is more than ten times that of the solid–liquid system. Moreover, the iron doping rate in the liquid–liquid system reaches 20.9 %, which is more than twice that of the solid–liquid system. The reason is that the high energy density of immersion ultrasound leads to emulsifying in the liquid–liquid system. The contact area between the two reactant phases and the number of reaction sites are greatly increased, resulting in a significant improvement in reaction efficiency and iron doping extent.

In addition, as seen from the gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Fig. S4) of the BTX before and after the reaction, there is almost no change in the peak position and height. It implies that the residual BTX is not contaminated. Therefore, the BTX after the reaction can be collected by centrifugation and then reused as the precursor, which is crucial for the industrialized preparation of Fe-CDs.

Fig. 3 a and Fig. S5 show the TEM images and particle size distributions of Fe-CDs prepared from liquid-liquid and solid–liquid systems. The results indicate that the size distribution of Fe-CDs from the solid–liquid system is in the range of 3–15 nm with an average particle size of 6 nm. The size distribution from the liquid–liquid system is in the range of 2–8 nm with an average particle size of 5.3 nm. It demonstrates that the coupling of immersed ultrasound with the liquid–liquid system can effectively decrease the size of the carbon dots and improve the uniformity of the particle size distribution.

Meanwhile, the HRTEM image of the carbon dots shows that the crystal plane spacing is 0.21 nm (Fig. 3a), which is attributed to the (211) lattice plane of Fe3C cores. The XRD spectra of the Fe-CDs are shown in Fig. S6. The positions of the cusp 2θ peaks are at 20.7°, 26.3°, and 31.4°, which can be assigned to the (101), (002), and (110) lattice planes of graphite, respectively. The locations of the spikes are at 34.3°, 41.0°, 47.6°, 50.0°, 52.0°, 53.6°, 56.8°, 57.5°, 72.3° and 74.5°, which are corresponding to the (111), (200), (201), (211), (031), (112), (131), (221), (222), (013) lattice planes of Fe3C (JCPDS No. 65-0393). In addition, the Fe-CDs under the liquid–liquid system have more obvious lattice fringes compared with the solid–liquid system. It indicates that the ultrasonic field not only enhances the mass transfer at the interface but also provides a localized high-temperature and high-pressure microenvironment. As a result, the iron doping rate in Fe-CD could be greatly enhanced, enabling them to exhibit higher catalytic activity.

As shown in Fig. 3b, the FT-IR spectra of Fe-CDs displays typical absorptions at 1615 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 (CC stretching of the aromatic ring), 1100 cm−1 and 1715 cm−1 (CO and C = respectively), 2925 cm−1 (methyl) and 578 cm−1 (sp2 graphitic carbon and/or Fe-O bond) at typical absorption [19] Raman spectra (Fig. 3c) show G (1583 cm−1, sp2 hybridization) and D (1340 cm−1, sp3 hybridization) of Fe-CDs. In addition to G and D peaks, a monolayered carbon structure (single Lorentzian peak) 2D peak (2700 cm−1) is also observed. The ratio of D peak to G peak (I_D/I_G) of the liquid–liquid system is 1.52, which is lower than that of the solid–liquid system (1.69) [15]. It indicates that the Fe-CDs synthesized by the liquid–liquid system have better crystallinity, which is conducive to the electron transfer and the catalytic reaction.

The elemental analysis and chemical composition of the fabricated Fe-CDs are analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3d, the XPS spectra of Fe-CDs show significant characteristic peaks for three elements, C1s (284 eV), O1s (533 eV), and Fe2p (710 eV) The calculated XPS elemental atomic compositions are 46.55 % C, 37.88 % O, and 15.58 % Fe. The high-resolution C1s spectra displays four distinct characteristic peaks, 284.7 eV, 285.3 eV, 287.1 eV, and 288.5 eV. The four distinct characteristic peaks attributed to the four functional groups CC, CC, CO and OCO (Fig. S7a) [20]. The higher fraction of CC (about 75.6 %) indicates a higher degree of graphitization on the surface of the material [21,22]. The XPS pattern of Fe-CD shows that the source of oxygen is air and water (as shown in Table S3 and Fig. S3). The binding energy peaks of Fe2p in Fe-CDs are located at 709.8 eV, 713.7 eV, 723.0 eV, and 726.0 eV (Fig. S7c). The XPS data are in good agreement with the FTIR results, which further confirms that iron ions have achieved effective doping on CDs [23]. Meanwhile, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) determined that the Fe content in the synthesized Fe-CDs was 20.9 %.

Iron doping changed the surface structure of the carbon dots, which is conducive to the improvement of the electron-giving and electron-accepting abilities of the carbon dots. This phenomenon is also reported by previous researchers. The Fe-CDs had higher utilization of active sites than other M−CDs (ie., Mn, Co, Ni, Cu, etc.) and exhibit superior catalytic properties [24,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of ultrasonic energy on synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To study the influence of the ultrasonic field on the formation of Fe-CDs, synthetic experiments with different ultrasonic powers (from 0 W to 1000 W with an interval of 200 W) are carried out. When the ultrasonic power is 0 W, no formation of Fe-CDs is observed. This further confirms that the ultrasonic field is essential for the synthesis of Fe-CDs by providing a high-temperature, high-pressure reaction microenvironment. Fig. 4 shows the FWHM (Full Width at Half Maximum) and the particle size of Fe-CDs with different ultrasonic powers. With the increase of ultrasonic power, the average particle size of Fe-CDs gradually decreases from 5.8 nm to 5.3 nm (the detailed TEM images are shown in Fig. S8), and the fluorescence emission gradually blue-shifts from 530 nm to 430 nm (the Fluorescence profiles is shown Fig. S1b). It means that the ultrasound has good regulation on the particle size in the liquid–liquid system. Furthermore, the FWHM decreases from 218.9 ± 19.7 nm to 100.5 ± 3.9 nm. This further indicates that ultrasound can improve the homogeneity of Fe-CDs in liquid–liquid systems.

To reveal the influence mechanism of ultrasound, the effects of fundamental wave energy, transient cavitation energy, and steady-state cavitation energy on the yield and properties of Fe-CDs are analyzed. Fig. 5 a shows the variation of yield of Fe-CDs and the various ultrasonic energy under different ultrasonic powers. With the increase of ultrasonic power from 0 W to 800 W, the yield of Fe-CDs gradually increases and reaches the maximum 159.7 ± 2.6 mg at 800 W., and then decreases to 143.2 ± 3.1 mg with the ultrasonic power further increasing to 1000 W. To reveal the reasons for this phenomenon, the correlation between the yields of the Fe-CDs and the ultrasonic energy was further investigated.

Fig. S9 and Table. S4 illustrate the Pearson correlation coefficients between the yield of Fe-CDs and the fundamental wave energy, steady-state cavitation energy, and transient cavitation energy. The results indicate that the correlation coefficients between the yield and the transient cavitation energy are the highest (0.98). This is quite reasonable because only transient cavitation can provide a localized high-temperature and high-pressure micro-environment for the reaction [26]. Moreover, the high-speed micro-jet generated during the collapse of the transient cavitation bubbles can break up the droplets. As a result, the specific surface area is increased and the exchange of substances at the liquid–liquid interface is accelerated,which strengthens the mass-transfer [27,28].

The distribution of ultrasonic cavitation energy is measured at different input powers to explore the relationship between the yield of Fe-CDs and transient cavitation energy, as shown in Fig. 6 . The red color indicates the high-energy zone and the blue color indicates the low-energy zone. The intensity of the total energy gradually increases with the increase of ultrasonic power, and the uniformity becomes better as shown in Fig. 6a. The transient cavitation energy gradually increases with the ultrasonic power increasing to 800 W, as shown in Fig. 6b. When the ultrasonic power further increases to 1000 W, the increase of transient cavitation energy becomes insignificant and the uniformity of the transient cavitation energy distribution deteriorates compared with that at 800w. This is mainly because the excessive ultrasonic energy at 1000 W generates more cavitation bubbles including numerous steady-state bubbles, which impedes the propagation of ultrasonic energy. It means that the yield of Fe-CDs depends both on the energy level of the transient cavitation and correlates with its spatial distribution.

Fig. 5b shows the FWHM of Fe-CDs and the ultrasonic energy under different ultrasonic powers. As the ultrasonic power increases, the FWHM of Fe-CDs decreases, which improve the homogeneity of Fe-CDs. FWHM−1 serves as the homogeneity evaluation index, which can demonstrate the effects of the ultrasonic field on the homogeneity of Fe-CDs. Table S4 shows the Pearson correlation coefficients of FWHM−1 with fundamental wave energy, steady-state cavitation energy, and transient cavitation energy for Fe-CDs. The results indicate that the positive correlation between FWHM−1 and transient cavitation energy up to 0.96. The collapse of the transient and steady-state cavitation bubbles generates microjets. Owing to the periodic oscillations of the microjets, the mixing and mass transfer efficiency between the reactants are enhanced, leading to the improvement of the Fe-CDs homogeneity. It connotes that the transient cavitation intensity is a crucial factor influencing the Fe-CDs homogeneity. In summary, the acoustic chemical effects generated by transient cavitation can effectively drive the reaction. Transient cavitation is the core factor affecting both the yield and the homogeneity of Fe-CDs. The uniformity of acoustic energy distribution determines the homogeneity of Fe-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasound on droplets and mass transfer
ce:section.ce:para: The transient cavitation effects of the ultrasonic field provide a high-temperature, high-pressure acoustic-chemical microenvironment for the reaction. The droplet size decreases and the contact area increases, which enhances the mass transfer between the two phases [29]. The droplets sizes under varying ultrasonic power are analyzed in order to evaluate the effects of ultrasound on droplets and mass transfer. As shown in Fig. 7 , the droplet particle size decreases and the size distribution becomes narrower with the increasing of ultrasound power. Without sonication, the average microdroplet size is 200 ± 17.3 μm. After ultrasonic treatment, the average size of the microdroplets decreases to the minimum at 800 W (8.1 ± 2.9 μm), which is two orders of magnitude smaller than the original droplets. The specific surface area of the droplets is greatly increases, which provides more interfacial synthesis reaction sites. This is because the higher ultrasonic power generates more cavitation intensity, leading to the more violent droplet separation [30,31]. The material yield of Fe-CDs increases from 0.7 ± 0.1 % to 11.9 ± 0.2 % due to the more reaction sites. As a result, ultrasonic power can effectively regulate the reaction process by controlling the size and distribution of droplets. Small and uniform droplets produced at high power are helpful to accurately control the reaction of Fe-CDs.

In general, the reduction of the droplet volume improves the synthesis by promoting the mass transfer process. To explore this enhancement, we investigate the mass transfer coefficient Kla was investigated. The following equation is used to calculate the Kla for the process[32,33]:
Kla =
1 τ
ln
C i
-
C α
C o
-
C α

Where, τ represents the average residence time, while Ci , Co , and Cα represent the initial, final, and equilibrium concentrations of ferric ions, respectively. A higher Kla indicates a more efficient mixing of the reactants, resulting in a faster and more effective synthesis process. As shown in Fig. 7, the mass transfer coefficient Kla increases with ultrasonic power increasing to 800 W. Kla increased by 252 % at 800 W, but it decreases as the ultrasonic power further increases to 1000 W. This is because the mass transfer is weakened by the excessive cavitation at 1000 W.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic properties of Iron-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: 1,4-DHP is a crucial component of many biologically active compounds and serves as an excellent substrate for preparing pyridine derivatives which has widely applied in drugs, herbicides, and insecticides [34–36]. Photocatalytic oxidation of 1,4-DHP are selected as the model reaction which are detected by UV/vis absorption spectra (Fig. 8 a). This oxidation reaction is conducted under an air atmosphere with a 35 W xenon lamp from 0 to 90 mins. In the absence of photocatalysts or light, there are no significant change in UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. S10). It indicates that the photocatalysts and light irradiation are essential for the photo-oxidation reaction of 1,4-DHP.

After adding carbon dots, the characteristic absorption of 1,4-DHP at 370 nm is reduced, while the absorption of pyridine derivatives at 280 nm is increased (Fig. 8b). After 90 min of light irradiation, the conversion rate of 1,4-DHP catalyzed by Fe-CDs-800 reaches up to 98.2 %, which surpasses the conversion rate by Fe-CDs-600 (81.2 %) and Fe-CDs-1000 (87.9 %), as show in Fig. 8c and S11. These results also outperformed those obtained from iron doped carbon dots synthesized by other methods, including hydrothermal synthesis (85.5 %) and thermal cracking (76.9 %), as well as other metal-doped carbon dots like Ni-CDs (91.1 %) and Cu-CDs (56.4 %) [37]. The comparison of all carbon points is shown in the Table S5. It indicates that the carbon dots synthesized by 800 W ultrasound in liquid–liquid system are more favorable for photocatalytic reactions.

In addition, the photocatalytic durability of Fe-CDs was investigated. At the end of each cycle, the catalyst was separated by Centrifugation, washed with ethanol, and the residual solvent was removed by vacuum drying. After five cycles, the catalyst is still able to achieve 98.7 % conversion. The catalyst is further analyzed using XPS and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S12), and there is no significant change in the spectra and components of the catalyst after several cycles, indicating that the catalyst does not undergo degradation.

To enable the photooxidation of 1,4-DHP, it is crucial for photocatalysts to possess electron donating and accepting abilities. Fe-CDs synthesized using high-density ultrasound coupled with liquid–liquid interface are evaluated for these properties. The electron acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) with a potential of −0.9 V vs. NHE and the electron donor N, N-diethylaniline (DEA) with a potential of 0.88 V vs. NHE were utilized. The Stern-Volmer plot is shown in Fig. 8d, 8e, where the emission intensity varies with the concentration of the quencher agent (0–0.003 M).

Among these catalyst, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (slope) of Fe-CDs-800 W is significantly larger than that of Fe-CDs-600 W and Fe-CDs-1000 W (Fig. S13), indicating that Fe-CDs-800 W has the strongest electron-donating ability (Fig. S13d). It can be attributed to the higher iron doping rates in CDs. Besides, the intensity of DEA quenching emission at different concentrations is tested to evaluate the electron accepting ability of Fe-CDs (Fig. S14). The results show that the electron accepting ability of Fe-CDs-800 W is significantly better than that of Fe-CDs-600 W and Fe-CDs-1000 W. Compare with the structure of these Fe-CDs, the higher iron doping rate (20.9 %) in Fe-CDs-800w significantly enhances their electron transfer ability, including both electron donating and accepting abilities for improved photocatalytic performance.

This work proposes a new approach to efficiently prepare Fe-CDs using a liquid–liquid system that is enhanced by high-density ultrasound at normal temperature and pressure. The research results show that the instantaneous collapse of cavitation bubbles provides proper microenvironment for the preparation of Fe-CDs. Coupling the ultrasonic field with the liquid–liquid reaction system significantly reduces the size of Fe-CDs and improves their particle size distribution homogeneity. The reaction yield is increased by over ten times (from 0.7 ± 0.1 % to 11.9 ± 0.2 %) and the iron doping rate is doubled (20.9 %). It is found that the transient cavitation energy is the main factor in strengthening the yield and homogeneity of Fe-CDs. Additionally, the cavitation effects coupling with acoustic flows lead to the decrease in droplet size and the increase in the contact area of the two-phase reactants. Thus, the synergistic effect of transient cavitation and the liquid–liquid reaction system is a key factor to promote synthesis of Fe-CDs. Benefiting from these, Fe-CDs-800 W has a strong electron donating and accepting abilities, which shows a photocatalytic oxidation conversion of 1,4-DHP up to 98.2 %. In this research, we employ a combination of ultrasound and liquid–liquid interfaces to modulate the structure and properties of Fe-CDs. This method synthesis Fe-CDs with enhance photocatalytic abilities and superior electron transfer capabilities compared to those prepared by other methods. This approach is anticipated to facilitate the high-quality and scalable production of metal-doped carbon dots, including but not limit to Fe-CDs.

Zongbo Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shiliang Song: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yan Ding: Validation, Data curation. Jianhai Yu: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Wenting Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jin Li: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Xin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yong Guo: Visualization, Conceptualization. Liang Gong: Resources, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely appreciate financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52376084, 52276090, 22322815 and 22179146), the Young Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (tsqn20182027), the National Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Provincial (ZR2023MB026), the Natural Science Foundation of Qingdao (23-2-1-247-zyyd-jch).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.05.070.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
